data.title: Nutanix: Strong ARR Expansion, But Deceleration Is Likely To Disappoint (Upgrade)
data.description:
IT infrastructure stocks are having a moment in 2025. Infrastructure deals are what have propped up stocks like Oracle (ORCL) to fresh all-time highs, as businesses spend heavily on capex to ensure they are ready to run AI applications.

Nutanix (NASDAQ:NTNX) has enjoyed its fair share of this demand as well. The hybrid cloud software company, which specializes in "hyperconverged" infrastructure that allows businesses to run cloud-like IT environments within their own private data centers, has seen its share price surge >30% since the start of the year. But with valuations heating up, we have to take a reality check: Can Nutanix really keep rallying further?

I last wrote a sell article on Nutanix in May, when the stock was trading at similar levels in the high $70s. Since then, Nutanix saw a sharp correction in the summer, followed by an equally sharp rebound rally. While I do acknowledge that Nutanix has delivered strong performance in an otherwise tough macroeconomic climate, I think the stock's ascent back above $80 signals its valuation is getting overheated against the company's own expectations for growth to decelerate. I'm upgrading the stock to neutral given strong recent demand trends, but I don't see any compelling reasons to keep riding Nutanix's rally higher.

Since valuation is core to my skittishness for Nutanix, let's start with a discussion of where Nutanix's latest valuation multiples sit against its newly issued outlook for FY26, which is the year ending in July 2026.

At current share prices near $81, Nutanix trades at a market cap of $21.75 billion. Netting off the $994.4 million of cash and $570.1 million of convertible debt on Nutanix's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $21.32 billion.

Meanwhile, as shown in the chart above, for FY26, Nutanix has initiated guidance of $2.90-$2.94 billion, which represents just 14-16% y/y growth. It's also guiding to $790-$830 million in free cash flow, or 8% growth and a 28% FCF margin at the midpoint (-2 points versus a 30% margin in FY25, which the company attributes to a number of large customers pre-paying multi-year deals in FY25). This puts Nutanix's valuation multiples at:

  • 7.3x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 26.3x EV/FY26 FCF

Nutanix essentially trades in line with a number of other software companies that have a similar mid-teens growth profile:

My core issue, however, is that Nutanix is a more commoditized product. Moreover, there's a strong chance that the current demand wave that Nutanix is enjoying is more temporal in nature, as businesses look to rapidly upgrade their infrastructure to quickly deploy AI capabilities. In other words, new business drove much of Nutanix's growth in FY25, but going forward, it's going to have to rely much more on renewals, and specifically upsells during renewals, to drive growth. The second note in the company's guidance commentary already warns investors that the renewal cohort in FY26 will continue to grow y/y, but at a slower rate than in FY25. In other words, deceleration is likely here. This will make it hard for Nutanix to stand toe-to-toe with other high-growth software stocks and sustain its ~7x revenue multiple, when in the past it was treated more like a commodity IT product at a ~4-5x revenue multiple.

All in all, I think the market's enthusiasm for IT infrastructure stocks this year has lifted Nutanix to newfound stardom, but I'm skeptical that this enthusiasm will continue to hold as Nutanix's growth rates taper off and decelerate. Take advantage of the year-to-date rally to lock in gains, and don't get too greedy on this stock.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Nutanix's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Nutanix's revenue grew 19% y/y to $653.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $643.0 million (+17% y/y) by a two-point margin. That being said, we note that growth decelerated three points versus 22% y/y growth in Q3. And with Nutanix guiding to just 14-16% growth in FY26, we can only expect to see deceleration from here on out.

Meanwhile, the company added $80 million of net-new ARR in the quarter to end at $2.22 billion. Net revenue retention clocked in at 108%, indicating 8% net expansion, weakening two points versus 110% in Q3.

New customer wins were a highlight in all of FY25, and the company ended FY26 with 29.3k customers, up 11% y/y. Management notes that new customer pickups have been a strong point for Nutanix amid a more challenging macroeconomic environment. It still expects some choppiness in certain sectors moving forward, including and especially the U.S. public sector. Per CFO Rukmini Sivaraman's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

We continue to see strength in landing new customers, and we are grateful for the over 2,700 customers who joined our platform this year. This included organizations of various sizes and across several industry verticals, and included over 50 Global 2000 accounts, a meaningful increase year-over-year [...]

One, we expect to continue landing new customers onto our platform at a rate of approximately mid- to high 3 digits of new logos a quarter in fiscal year '26. We also expect some continued uncertainty in the overall macro environment, including in areas such as U.S. federal government spending and with regard to currency fluctuations.

Two, we expect the renewals ACV cohort or the available to renew pool in fiscal year '26 to grow year-over-year, but at a slower pace than in fiscal year '25 as the overall renewal base gets larger over time."

Again, we emphasize that Nutanix will need to rely more and more on its install base customers to drive expansion deals upon renewal. This may be tough to bank on, as customers are front-loading their capex requirements upfront in order to get AI applications up and running, but this doesn't mean Nutanix will be able to sustain growth past current inflated levels.

On the profitability front, we note that Nutanix has achieved meaningful margin expansion. Pro forma operating margins jumped 5 points y/y to 18%, very nearly putting Nutanix in "Rule of 40" territory.

And as shown in the chart above, FCF of $750.2 million in FY25 grew 26% y/y and hit a 30% margin, 2 points higher y/y.

Key takeaways

Nutanix remains a "high price for high quality" software investment that I think will underperform the broader markets as investors push back against elevated valuation multiples. Investors should recognize here that Nutanix may be at the top of a demand peak, benefiting from its customers' rush to equip their data centers to run AI applications—and from here on out, the company itself is expecting meaningful growth deceleration. Maintain caution here from the sidelines.

data.title: Oracle: Backlog Is A Distraction, Exit Before The Bubble Bursts
data.description:
When we see a bullish rally of epic proportions playing out in the market, the first instinct should not be to blindly follow the momentum but to investigate what narrative is exactly being priced in. Oftentimes, shareholders end up losing pretty significantly when they fall for an unsustainable narrative being pushed by the management.

To me, this currently seems to be the case with Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL). Oracle has historically positioned itself as an enterprise IT solutions provider and has built a global reputation on databases, middleware, and enterprise software systems that had, at one stage, become the backbone of corporate IT infrastructure.

Now with the AI proliferation boom in full swing, Oracle is increasingly reorienting itself as a cloud and AI infrastructure player. This shift represents a dramatic attempt to reframe its legacy position in the enterprise stack into something aligned with the hottest growth trend in markets today. Of course, this signals a massive opportunity for the company.

Following its recent Q1 results, which saw a double miss, despite decent growth, the stock has surged by nearly 36% in a single session. This has taken the company's market cap from ~$670 billion to almost $1 trillion. The bulk of the excitement seems to hinge on the narrative of Oracle’s massive backlog and its positioning as a central hub for AI workloads, with remaining performance obligations amounting to almost half a trillion dollars.

In this analysis, I argue that a closer examination of this narrative reveals quite some significant structural fragility beneath the surface. As such, I strongly believe that the current run-up, which has massively inflated the stock's premiums, rests on a very flimsy foundation.

As such, I think ORCL is trading abnormally above its intrinsic value and am therefore issuing a Strong Sell rating on the stock.

Overview of the Quarter & Major Developments

Oracle reported its FY26 Q1 results earlier this week, and the market response has been quite solid, considering that the stock surged by as much as 36% in a single day. Given such undeniable bullish sentiment, let's dive into the actual results and investigate what truly drove this reaction.

The overall top line for the quarter amounted to $14.9 billion, which is 12.2% higher than the Q1 total in FY25 of $13.3 billion. In particular, the Cloud segment of Oracle's business grew by 27.7% (YoY) to $7.2 billion, overtaking the Software business to become Oracle's largest revenue-earning segment. The main growth driver for this was the cloud infrastructure business, which jumped by an impressive 54% YoY to $3.3 billion, while cloud database services jumped by 43% YoY to $2.8 billion.

Interestingly enough, while the topline has seen an impressive surge, the company's gross margin during the quarter has actually shrunk by 337 basis points (YoY), from 70.94% to 67.28%. This, of course, is because the company is still in a Capex-intensive phase of scaling out its infrastructure footprint and spent over $8.5 billion this quarter in capital expenditure. For context, that's about 65% of the total revenue brought in this quarter.

Net income, during this quarter, remained almost unchanged at $2.93 billion, compared to the prior year's Q1 bottom line figure. While the total had remained the same in the last 12 months, net income margin actually fell by 240 basis points (YoY), from 22.01% to 19.61%.

It must be pointed out that the company actually missed expectations on both the top and bottom lines by underperforming 0.78% on revenue and 0.62% on EPS. Despite this, the market responded with overwhelming enthusiasm, which would seem perplexing to many without the full picture.

This is where the company's massive backlog update comes into play. Management reported in its recent earnings call that its remaining performance obligations [RPO] ballooned dramatically by ~360% to $455 billion. That's nearly half a trillion dollars. The management has emphasized that this "booked" revenue will convert as additional capacity keeps coming online.

Based on this, it's hard to argue against the investor confidence we see, because large and contracted backlogs typically de-risk future revenue and deliver better predictability to scaling roadmaps. In fact, this backlog update seems to be the most outstanding highlight of the company during this quarter and a big reason why the stock has rallied by as much as it has.

In the subsequent section, I will unpack why this backlog figure, while extremely headline-grabbing, actually deserves a far more cautious interpretation.

Oracle’s Backlog Narrative as a Distraction

Typically, when a company falls short on near-term targets, the management usually shifts to amplifying longer-term commitments as a means to reassure its shareholders. I suspect this to be the case with Oracle. Because the company missed both its top and bottom-line expectations, it's engaging in a narrative pivot with an unusual emphasis on its five-year backlog. In fact, it is actually not unlikely for multi-year commitments to be re-negotiated, or even deferred entirely if external conditions undergo material shifts. As such, treating RPOs as locked-in revenue could be a serious mistake.

I personally believe that market participants should be somewhat skeptical of the backlog hype, especially because not only is backlog not exactly revenue, but it is also heavily reliant on a number of assumptions. In Oracle's case, some of these assumptions are actually quite fragile.

For one, Oracle's backlog is actually tied to the growth (and spending) trajectory of OpenAI. This is because these contracts are typically consumption-based, which means that Oracle only realizes this "booked" revenue as OpenAI actually uses capacity. For this to happen, OpenAI would need to secure the funds to sustain increased computing power as it grows.

Herein lies the fragility of this backlog. OpenAI is highly capital-intensive and also unprofitable. This means it would need to continuously fundraise to continue growing. Without a durably profitable business model, any slowdown in capital inflows would mean that infrastructure spending would have to be curtailed. If this were to play out, a massive chunk of Oracle's backlog would instantly vanish.

In fact, following OpenAI's $300 billion pact with Oracle, over 90% of Oracle’s future contracts are tied to OpenAI, creating enormous customer concentration. This is quite problematic, especially since the $300 billion compute contract actually dwarfs OpenAI's current revenue base. This, as such, raises serious questions about the sustainability of these commitments.

Having such a degree of dependence on a single customer turning profitable is bad enough, but Oracle's situation seems even more complicated than this. Oracle's GPU-centric cloud business is also heavily reliant on NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) supplying and renting out its GPUs for Oracle's infrastructure layer. This is a precarious dynamic because Nvidia is not only a critical supplier to the company but also happens to be a competitor to the company. If in the future Nvidia decides to amend the volume it funnels to customers like Oracle in order to capture more of the economics for itself, Oracle's backlog would further lose credibility.

Overall, this points to a deeper structural issue with Oracle, which is that the company doesn't hold a meaningful hardware advantage. In fact, the majority of its AI demand seems to be won by its positioning itself as a cheaper and faster alternative to other hyperscalers. As such, banking on the backlog narrative without a consideration of these more structural issues could backfire pretty hard.

Valuation Assessment

Following its earnings, ORCL stock has seemingly taken on a life of its own. In the premarket trading session, it climbed by as much as 32% to $340. As a result, its market cap has rocketed from $670 billion to nearly $950 billion.

This, of course, creates quite the spectacle, as it means Oracle could very well be joining the trillion-dollar tech titans if this trend continues. While this may be exciting to watch, especially for those who have had a good entry on the stock, I believe that bulls may be getting a little carried away here.

I understand that, following the company's recent earnings, there is strong optimism surrounding Oracle, especially given the strides it's made towards capturing AI workloads and building a formidable backlog. Even if we ignore the backlog fragility argument I have discussed in the previous section, I think we can also recognize that Oracle's stock price has run far ahead of its fundamentals.

Let me explain by numbers.

At the time of writing this article, Oracle is trading at the $340 mark, which implies a market cap of ~$950 billion. A further 5% climb from here would take the company across the trillion-dollar point. The company's trailing twelve-month [TTM] earnings stand at around $12.4 billion, which means it holds a TTM P/E ratio of ~76.6x. That is, to be quite frank, heavily inflated, especially since the median P/E ratio seen in the tech sector is actually below 30.

I find this quite unjustifiable as the stock is expected to see its bottom line (or GAAP EPS) climb by 20% over the next 12 months, compared to the 11% growth expectation median seen in the broader sector. While that is impressive, it clearly does not justify a P/E ratio that is almost 160% higher than the sector median, in my opinion.

This recent run-up also skews the risk-reward dynamic to asymmetric proportions, with the upside potential being capped quite dramatically, while downside risk is far more pronounced, as the sky-high premiums imply sky-high expectations. If in the near future, Oracle fails to deliver epic proportions of top-line growth, we might see a sharp pullback in its price.

Once we factor in the backlog risks I have detailed above, we begin to realize that current premiums are based on an extremely flimsy foundation, with too much dependence on things not within Oracle's sphere of control.

Given this broader picture, I am rating Oracle as a strong sell.

Risk to Thesis

One risk to the thesis I have presented above is my assumption that Oracle is not and will not engage in meaningful efforts to diversify its backlog and customer base. I could, of course, very well be proven wrong, as I'm sure the management might be quite aware of the vulnerable position it currently stands in. If Oracle does decide to aggressively pursue new client relationships and onboarding a wider range of enterprise AI workloads, the concentration problem I highlighted becomes less relevant.

Moreover, considering that the AI proliferation boom is still far from its maturity, it might be wrong of me to assume OpenAI will be facing material problems in terms of sustaining its own growth trajectory. If OpenAI does in fact accelerate its commercialization of its products, Oracle's heavy reliance on the company could pay off big time. However, I believe banking on this is very risky for Oracle shareholders, hence my Strong Sell thesis.

Investor Takeaway

Oracle's recent bull rally that has taken it to the verge of a trillion-dollar market cap shows the power of narrative in driving market sentiment. While the company is in fact executing a bold pivot into cloud and AI infrastructure, it is important to be cautious of the picture-perfect story that management seems to be conveying. The nearly half-trillion-dollar backlog comes with too many caveats to be treated as guaranteed revenue.

I believe the current price is far above Oracle's intrinsic value, and, therefore, selling now would be the wisest option to go with.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Market Variables Are Still Soft, But Growth Prospects Are Solid
data.description:
About four months after my initial analysis, Dorchester Minerals, L.P. (NASDAQ:DMLP) continues to grapple with weak external factors. Yet, its operations remain fruitful amid its continued expansion and acquisition. This is supported by its robust liquidity position that promises the longevity of cash distributions to its unitholders. It has also become severely undervalued after its continued dip, although I understand the pessimistic market stance today. Technicals are still weak but reveal new buying opportunities.

DMLP: Growth Is Challenged, But It Is Very Fruitful And Sustainable

In the last few months, energy prices have weakened amid stubborn inflation, softer consumer demand, and OPEC oil hikes. There were some sudden upticks during the Israel-Iran tension, but these were very short-lived to offset the impact of the price downtrend. Dorchester Minerals, LP felt the impact of weak external factors, especially during the second quarter.

Its operating revenue amounted to $32.4M, down by -13.4% YoY from $37.4M. It was also -1.2% weaker than in my previous coverage at $32.8M. These were mainly due to price-demand-supply dynamics in the oil and gas markets. If we look at it on a YoY basis, sales volume increased overall. Yet, the average sales price dropped and it was easily offset. As we can see, WTI Crude went below $60 per barrel. This was its four-year low during the second quarter. In turn, its royalties and net profit share also decreased. The same happened to natural gas after falling below $3.00 per MMBtu.

Despite this, DMLP worked on its efficiency to offset the impact of lower revenues. If not for the sudden increase in depreciation and amortization, margins would have been relatively stable. This can be shown by its operating cash flow, which increased and allowed it to cover a huge portion of its dividend payments. With that, its cash level remains relatively stable and adequate. DMLP is still generating enough earnings or cash inflows to sustain its operating capacity and dividends. It does not have to rely on borrowings to support its operations. It is very liquid and sustainable.

Oil Will Still Be A Double-Edged Sword

WTI remains weak, ranging at $60-65 today. While its price trend has already improved from Q2 2025, I think it will still take some time to rebound to $70 per barrel. Global inflation remains stubborn, which continues to affect consumption and production, and thus the demand for oil. In addition, OPEC maintains its cautious stance with its larger-than-expected oil supply hike last month. Now, the consolation is that OPEC members will reduce their supply hike to 137,000 barrels per day next month. The price is now about $1 higher than last week. But I won’t be expecting much change in the price unless another tension in the Middle East breaks out or OPEC halts its supply hike. With that, the oil sales price will remain at its current range.

On a lighter note, this can make the oil price trend more predictable and stable. Also, lower oil prices can boost demand since this also means lower fuel prices for various industries, such as freight and logistics, shipping, airlines, restaurants, and land transportation services. The potential increase in volume can still partially offset the impact of lower oil prices. While sales may still be weaker on a YoY basis, its quarterly trend may remain stable for the remainder of the year.

Natural Gas: Price Remains Weak, But Growth Prospects Are Still Rosy

In recent months, natural gas has weakened further as the price dropped to $2.74 three weeks ago. While it has already gone back to $3.05, the price remains weak compared to the previous quarter. The consolation is that it remains much stronger on a YoY basis. Also, it must be noted that natural gas demand is much more sensitive to price changes than oil. I also expect seasonal volume spikes in Q3 due to Summer. This can help DMLP stabilize its royalties and net profits interest in natural gas.

In the long run, we see attractive growth prospects for natural gas. Natural gas is still the primary source of electricity consumption in the world, especially in the US. And I think it will remain that way despite the calls for renewable sources. After all, natural gas is still more scalable than renewables, easier to produce and more accessible than coal, and much safer than nuclear energy. Data centers are at the forefront of the electricity consumption surge in the future as the digital revolution persists. With the increasing need for internet access, more data centers may be built, which will require more electricity and increase the demand for natural gas.

In a recent study, data center vacancy has already dropped to 6.6%, showing that demand continues to outpace supply and/or capacity. This is why global data center pricing has already increased by an average rate of 3.3% to $217.30 per kW every month. Considering these numbers, global data center capacity may expand by 15% every year.

It Remains Well-Positioned

DMLP remains well-positioned against oil and natural gas price volatility with its business model. Its own business model is also its insulation since it earns royalties from mineral rights. This ensures a constant revenue stream, although it varies with the sales or revenues of oil producers/drillers. Aside from that, it receives net profits interests and occasional one-time payments or lease bonuses from its mineral rights lessees. As a result, its business model is also well diversified. Most importantly, its business model is not cost-sensitive and capital-intensive since it only owns mineral rights. It does not drill, explore, or produce oil and natural gas. It doesn’t need to worry about drilling equipment, storage facilities, and pipelines. So, it can easily control its costs and cash outflows.

It can easily expand by just buying mineral interests. It can offer common units to the owners to acquire mineral rights. An example is its acquisition of mineral interests of about 3,050 net royalty acres in Adams County, Colorado in exchange for 915,694 common units or interests in DMLP. While this means higher cash distribution outflows, DMLP was able to expand without bleeding out millions or billions at once. It did not have to borrow, which can be risky amid uncertain economic trends.

Moreover, this acquisition increases its revenue streams. It also positions it well for more opportunities, especially in natural gas.

Valuation

DMLP has already declined by -14% from my previous coverage despite our optimistic view of this stock. I understand the pessimistic and/or cautious stance of the market as energy prices remain weak. However, it is indeed an opportunity to buy this stock at a discount. After all, DMLP remains well-positioned with new growth catalysts to justify its upside in the long run. This is still much lower than my DCF Model TP of $37.92 in my previous coverage. Even if I add a 10% risk consideration to lower the TP to $34.12, the stock is still cheap today.

To confirm this, I also used price ratios. When I checked its historical trend, I noticed its sensitivity to the EPS and cash per share, which should not be surprising. Using P/E, the stock only trades at 14.42x its EPS versus the five-year average of 19.23x. It is also below the median of 14.95x. If we use the average to derive its fair value, the TP will be $32.89. If we use the median multiple, the TP will be $25.57. Either way, the stock is cheap. The same goes for P/CPS, as the stock trades at only 31.60x, which is lower than the average of 41.50x and the median of 35.39x. Using these, the TP will be $32.38 and $27.60, respectively.

I also used the Dividend Discount Model for a more forward-looking approach. For its annualized dividends, I used $3.08 (the sum of payouts in the past four quarters) and $2.48 ($0.62 in four quarters). For its dividend growth rate, I just used the average dividend growth rate since 2022 to remove the impact of the pandemic. Since there are two annualized dividends, their average dividend growth rates are also different. Meanwhile, I estimated its cost of equity using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. For the risk-free rate, I just set it at the standard rate of 5% for simplicity. Lastly, I estimated Beta by comparing the daily price change of the stock versus the S&P 500 in the past three years using the SLOPE function on MS Excel. As you can see, there is a huge difference between the two TPs. Yet, they are both higher than the current price, proving that the stock is still reasonably valued.

Technicals

DMLP’s momentum is still weak as the price continues to create lower highs and lower lows. The price trend is still lower than the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending downward, which means that recent market sentiments are still pessimistic.

However, MACD and RSI show new entry opportunities. The MACD line is still in negative territory but increasing above the signal line. This is supported by the histogram within positive territory. These two indicators show that buying volume has recently increased and exceeded selling volume. This can tell us that many investors found the recent dip a solid support level. This can be supported by its RSI, which reveals recent overselling. Momentum has improved but remains far below the overbought region, so there is still enough room for buying.

Conclusion

Dorchester Minerals, LP remains sensitive to the oil and gas market amid its weaker prices and revenues. But it stays well-positioned against market headwinds and for growth catalysts. This justifies its upside potential aside from its evident underpricing. Technicals support it as new buying opportunities appear despite weak momentum. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Pyxis Oncology: Readouts Ahead, Although Cash Might Dwindle (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Oncology drug developer, Pyxis Oncology (NASDAQ:PYXS), has three readouts ahead from its work with micvotabart pelidotin in recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (r/m HNSCC). I rated PYXS a buy in September 2024, based on the fact that it had multiple readouts ahead, cash until H2'26, and a run up into those readouts could occur. This article looks at where PYXS stands now a year later, the readouts ahead, and why I now rate the name a hold.

Micvotabart pelidotin provides shots on goal

PYXS's micvotabart pelidotin (micvo, formerly PYX-201) is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting extra-domain B of fibronectin, a component of the extracellular matrix around tumor cells.

When the company reported data from a dose escalation trial of micvo in various tumor types in November 2024, the drug did demonstrate the ability to produce tumor shrinkage, including confirmed responses, across a range of tumor types.

What we can see from that data is that micvo certainly looked to offer efficacy in HNSCC, albeit the data came from just eight patients. I have to wonder if this study were run again, might another one of the cancer types have looked like a better option. For example if another 10 patients were enrolled in each of the sarcoma, HNSCC, ovarian cancer groups, would r/m HNSCC still be the best indication for further development of micvo? Of course PYXS can still develop micvo in those indications down the road, provided it has the funds to do so. Indeed the company notes it will select a dose of micvo for combination with pembrolizumab (an anti-PD-1) for further work in various tumor types in mid-2025.

What is clear was that tolerability of micvo was at least passable, if not pretty good. There were no grade 5 adverse events in the 77 patient safety population and only two treatment discontinuations. For example at 3.6 mg/kg micvo, the rate of grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events was 9% (1/11 patients, although at 5.4 mg/kg the rate was 36% (14/39 patients).

Since the late 2024 results from the dose escalation work with micvo, the company has been focused on r/m HNSCC. In H2'25 PYXS will present preliminary data from about 20 second- and third-line HNSCC patients treated with micvo, who had already taken a platinum-based chemotherapy, and a PD-1 blocker. The company will also present data in H1'26 from r/m HNSCC patients treated with micvo who had already taken an anti-EGFR antibody and a PD-1 blocker.

Combination work in first-line (and beyond) patients is also on tap for H2'25, so with another 20 patients or so expected from that group, we might get data from another 40 HNSCC patients in H2'25, which is a sizable update from the original eight patients.

Some competition in HNSCC

Tivdak

One of the issues with pursuing HNSCC is the competition in that space. Notably, in June 2024, Genmab A/S (GMAB) announced results from Part C of the innovaTV 207 trial in 40 patients with r/m HNSCC treated with tisotumab vedotin (Tivdak), a tissue factor-targeting ADC. Tivdak is already approved in cervical cancer. GMAB reported a 32.5% response rate in the 40 HNSCC patients in Part C of the innovaTV 207 trial, including one complete response.

The status of Tivdak in r/m HNSCC is unclear, it appears Pfizer (PFE) and GMAB decided not to go ahead with a trial in second- and third-line HNSCC patients (page 9), but the innovaTV 207 trial includes Part F, which includes first-line r/m HNSCC patients. Notably, in Part C, the response rate was higher in second- and third-line r/m HNSCC patients (10/25 patients responded, 40% ORR, slide 9), suggesting Tivdak works better in earlier lines of therapy (which isn't surprising). As such, even greater activity could be seen in first-line r/m HNSCC in part F of innovaTV 207, so we may not have seen the end of Tivdak in HNSCC.

Petosemtamab

Elsewhere, Merus NV (MRUS) is developing its antibody petosemtamab (an EGFR and LGR5 binding bispecific) in two phase 3 trials; one in first-line r/m HNSCC and another in second- or third-line r/m HNSCC. Indeed MRUS expects these trials to be substantially enrolled by the end of this year and produce a readout in 2026 (potentially from both trials). Notably, first line data in r/m HNSCC include a 63% response rate and 79% survival at 12 months when petosemtamab was used in combination with pembrolizumab, in patients with 1% or higher combined positive score (CPS) for PD-L1 expression.

Eftilagimod

For first-line r/m HNSCC patients with less than 1% CPS, Immutep Limited (IMMP) is developing eftilagimod alfa (or efti), a fusion protein targeting LAG-3 to activate an anti-tumor immune response. Results announced in May from cohort B of the TACTI-003 trial include a median overall survival of 17.6 months produced by a combination of efti in combination with pembrolizumab (N=31 patients). IMMP believes these data compare favorably to options like pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy, which produced an 11.3 month median OS in 39 patients in Keynote-048. Of course Keynote-048 is a larger study, but I can see potential for efti in HNSCC.

Financial Overview

PYXS finished Q2'25 with $88.9M in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities. The company also had a further $1.5M in restricted cash. PYXS did bring in $2.8M in milestone revenue in Q2'25 from Simcere Pharmaceutical, as Simcere gained approval in China of suvemcitug, which was originally developed by Apexigen, which PYXS acquired in 2023. PYXS is eligible for mid- to high-single digit royalties, but it isn't clear how suvemcitug will sell in China.

For Q2'25, PYXS reported; R&D expenses of $17.1M, G&A expenses of $5.4M and a net loss of $18.4M. Net cash used in operating activities was $39.8M in the first six months of 2025. At the current rate, PYXS would run out of cash in Q3'26, which fits with PYXS's guidance of cash lasting into H2'26.

There were 62,018,135 shares of PYXS's common stock outstanding as of August 13, 2025. PYXS has a market cap of $115.4M ($1.86 per share).

Risks

There are several risks to a long in PYXS, a few of which I'll note here.

Firstly, three readouts for PYXS can provide three chances to impress but also three chances to disappoint the market. Since micvo represents the bulk of the company's current efforts clinically, with development of PYX-106 having paused, disappointing results with micvo could see the stock fall particularly heavily.

Further, PYXS could impress with upcoming results from its work with micvo, but competitors might provide updates afterwards, meaning any rally on the back of strong micvo data might be at risk of being cut short.

Lastly, PYXS could try raising cash to make it beyond H2'25, but issuing stock (or options/warrants) might not be well received, even if it somewhat addresses concerns on cash. Notably, PYXS had $106.2M of potential at-the-market stock sales remaining under its $125M at-the-market facility set up by an S-3 filing in November 2022. PYXS could file another S-3 in November, or might look to make use of the current S-3 before it is likely to expire (November 2025).

Conclusions and rating

While micvo is clearly an active drug, including in HNSCC, I can't get a sense of just how active it is based on only eight patients of prior data in HNSCC. Further, PYXS only has about a year or so of cash remaining, and trades above its cash balance. I find PYXS to be high risk here, and even if micvo does show itself to be worthy of further development when the company produces results in H2'25 and H1'26, I do worry about a raise weighing the stock down. I think particularly strong results might be needed with micvo to offset concerns about competition and the funding that will be needed to develop the drug further. At the current time then, I rate PYXS a hold. I stop short of rating the name a sell, because the drug has a chance with both monotherapy and combination work readouts in H2'25, while the company at least still has some cash in hand.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lithium Argentina: Playing A Lithium Bottom
data.description:
The lithium market remains volatile as Chinese supply fluctuates. Lithium Argentina AG (NYSE:LAR) is one of the more interesting ways to play ultimate lithium demand growth from EV expansion, due to the company already having an active mine. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock, especially after the recent dip.

Already Breakeven

As with most lithium miners, Lithium Argentina is a story about the future of lithium prices. The company is slightly more de-risked than a lot of other junior miners with a working lithium mine, but the investment story is still high risk.

Lithium Argentina reported Q2'25 sales of $64 million, as the company reached ~85% capacity at the Cauchari-Olaroz mine in Argentina. The miner is targeting 30K to 35K tonnes of lithium production this year.

The company is already generally breakeven with ~85,000 tonnes of quarterly production with the cost basis of $6,098/t. The production costs aren't the lowest in the lithium sector, but Lithium Argentina is low enough to have breakeven operations while the lithium prices are depressed.

During Q2'25, the company hit $7,400/t of lithium carbonate sold while the market had recently jumped back above $10,000 per tonne. Lithium prices had risen on CATL closing the key Jianxiawo lithium mine in China, but the sudden plans to restart the mine have hit lithium stocks and prices.

The biggest question is whether lithium prices hit a cyclical low in June. The market generally continues to propose a lithium shortfall as EV production moves forward around the world, and new lithium mines aren't opened fast enough due to the lower prices now.

Lithium Argentina is generally breakeven on a cash basis with lithium prices at the recent lows, as evidenced by the Q2 results with revenue of $64 million and cash costs of $55 million. The lithium miner becomes highly profitable on much higher lithium prices with the current production costs.

Big Future

The stock has a current market cap of just $500 million while the company targets 400% capacity growth. Lithium Argentina plans to increase production from 40,000 tpa to over 200,000 tpa via regional expansion and incorporating new processing technology being developed in China.

The easiest expansion plan is the 40,000 tpa Stage 2 plan at Cauchari-Olaroz to double the existing capacity to 80,000 tpa. The company only has a $68 million cash balance, so the expansion plans will have to be financed.

In addition, Lithium Argentina already plans a JV with Ganfeng to consolidate assets in the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes area of Argentina. Ganfeng’s solely owned Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes project, with Lithium Argentina’s Pastos Grandes project (85% owned) and the Sal de la Puna project (65% owned), will form the JV with plans to target 150,000 tpa of annual LCE production developed over 3 stages of 50,000 tpa.

While the expansion plans are great, the ideal scenario where investors make the most money is for lithium prices to soar before the company makes aggressive investments in new production. Lithium Argentina needs a far higher stock price and a related market cap before the company considers selling shares to raise capital, though Argentina has an incentive program to support further investment in the country.

The stock traded at $6 to $8 when the split with Lithium America (LAC) originally took place back in 2023. The company would have a market cap of over $1 billion in such a scenario, and raising more capital would be far more attractive.

Lithium Argentina has a revenue base in the $250 million range based on the lowest lithium prices in years. The stock only trades at 2x sales and is very attractive at this price, with any expansion plans that don't involve massive dilution.

The biggest risk is lithium prices continuing to fall to new lows, especially below production costs. Lithium Argentina is a small company with limited capital, and the politics in Argentina are always a risk, especially after President Javier Milei suffered an electoral defeat in recent local elections, questioning his authority to continue with more capitalist pursuits that would likely support lithium miners.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Lithium Argentina is an intriguing lithium mine investment. The company has an operating lithium mine and a list of assets to develop and expand mining output, supporting a much higher stock price if lithium prices rebound.

data.title: The Market Is Scaling The Wall Of Worry
data.description:
The Market Scaled The Wall Of Worry In H1

What a way to end August and start September. While investors awaited the proverbial September pullback/correction to arrive, the market wasted no time in printing new highs in the S&P 500 (SPX) (NYSEARCA:SPY) and Dow Jones, even as momentary weakness appeared to have crept into the Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ) (NDX). The solid Q2 earnings season has surprised Wall Street yet again, justifying another similar "hockey stick" growth inflection as observed in Q1.

Clearly, analysts have been found wanting for being too pessimistic about the earnings resilience of US companies. Yet, who could really blame them for having to dissect and digest the mixed signals emanating from the US economy as investors parse whether we could face a prolonged slowdown? The housing market has been under duress, while the job market has also stagnated. Yet, the ability for most companies to have outperformed the Q2 outlook underscores the underlying strength of US corporate productivity, even as the full force of AI-driven productivity boosts is still in its ascendancy.

Analysts Have Finally Awakened From Their Slumber

Nevertheless, what is starting to worry me is that analysts have finally started to get their act together, marking up the revenue growth estimates for the S&P 500 as a group as compared to their relative pessimism previously. In other words, while the market was assessed to be climbing a wall of worry as I highlighted in my last market update, could it be feasible that too many bulls are now starting to hop onto the bandwagon right now and popping their champagne?

Climax Top Signals Could Still Haunt Us

Truth to be told, climax top signals corroborating distribution are starting to show themselves more frequently in the Nasdaq. These are usually construed as early warning signs that validate early institutional selling that could precede a more pronounced selloff subsequently.

While they aren't intended to be market timing indicators, I believe a closer inspection of the market action that followed these occurrences over the past five years could prove instructive. The market has consistently topped out over the next few months, although the question of when remains to be seen.

Some of you may also point out that since Nasdaq is tech-heavy (54% exposure to the tech sector), a broadening advance might also help the previously underperforming sectors close the gap, potentially alleviating a selloff that might bring down the whole market.

Earnings Multiples Are Expensive

While I don't generally disagree with that thesis, I also think that we must be cognizant of the fact that valuation multiples are rich now, as compared to their long-term averages. The previous time the market reached similar levels in 2021, we then suffered a steep bear market that took almost a year before bottoming out in late 2022.

Yet, some may paint a rosier picture that we are still far from the 1999/2000 analog that saw earnings multiples surge to even higher levels before the melt-up ended in spectacular fashion, which culminated in a multi-year bear market.

Robust Earnings Arguably Support The Market's Optimism

Yet, I've maintained my bullish bias across the medium and long term, as I've informed my subscribers in my Investing Group: Ultimate Growth Investing. While I've observed selling pressure that may have affected the momentum of the Nasdaq vis-à-vis the S&P 500, it's critical to understand that the overall market isn't assessed to be fundamentally overvalued right now.

In other words, it seems plausible that robust and sustainable earnings accretion has been driving upside for the whole market, suggesting that the relatively high multiples are still predicated on strong ballast and not uncertain or wobbly foundations.

And with the Fed primed to lower interest rates by more than 5x (of 25 bps each) over the next 12 months (starting this September), the "invisible hand" prompted by the rate cuts could spur a melt-up phase through the end of the year or early 2026.

I'm not going to say that the coast is fully clear for all, because it's clearly not. Pressure from President Trump's tariffs could still impede the earnings outlook for some companies, although it seems like the overall impact has been less perilous than previously thought.

Also, we must be prepared for a potentially less independent Fed moving ahead that might have unforeseen consequences, imperiling the long-term stability of the financial markets. These uncertainties could still manifest in ways that may not have been fully contemplated as analysts decided to upgrade their outlook, following two quarters of being "behind the curve," so to speak.

data.title: Guggenheim's GOF Defies Closed-End Fund Logic, For A Reason (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (NYSE:GOF) is a closed-end fixed-income fund with a liberal mandate. It takes a go-anywhere approach to fixed income and it's trading at a 30.21% premium to net asset value. It utilizes leverage, its discount/premium to NAV and its great investment team to generate highly impressive returns. It has managed to do so over an extended period of time.

I rarely, if ever, buy CEFs at a premium to net asset value. I’ve generally published bearish reports on GOF over the years. Alternating between hold and sell. The oldest one I could find dates back to November 2020. There is a reason for this "policy".

If I buy at a discount, I'd like the sentiment around the CEF or its asset class to improve. When sentiment is terrible this tends to happen over time. Even if it improves to indifference that's fine. If I buy at a premium there is usually a lot of hype or optimism about the underlying or the manager. This doesn't tend to last very long.

Meanwhile the returns on GOF have been great:

GOF is one of the rare funds that appears to be able to retain its premium. This time I want to review one important factor that may (in part) explain why this happens. The fund has a built-in way to issue shares which is through its DRIP program. The DRIP program allows investors to receive the dividend in the shape of newly issued shares. It is explained in GOF’s annual report:

When shares trade at a discount to NAV, the DRIP takes advantage of the discount by reinvesting the monthly dividend distribution in common shares of the Fund purchased in the market at a price less than NAV. Conversely, when the market price of the Fund’s common shares is at a premium above NAV, the DRIP reinvests participants’ dividends in newly issued common shares at the greater of NAV per share or 95% of the market price per share.

If you have the DRIP turned on you are currently acquiring shares at only ~123% of net asset value. Reflecting 95% of the market price. Acquiring shares at a discount to the market price is obviously preferable to buying them in the open market. However, you’re also consistently investing at above market prices.

Personally, I’d turn this feature off at this large premium to net asset value.

It is advantageous if you expect the premium to last or rise. Something that’s been the case recently. The feature is also one reason you could argue in favor of remaining invested when you don't love the valuation. Especially if you expect other investors to continue investing at higher premiums than you’ve paid. Otherwise, it seems unwise to keep participating and buy shares at an ever greater premium while new shares are being issued continuously.

It seems great to buy a fund like this once it trades at a very attractive valuation (in terms of discount), turn off the drip once the premium to net asset value exceeds ~6% and then enjoy the additional value creation of shares being issued to other shareholders at large premia.

This drip feature coupled with the fund’s long and impressive track record does argue for holding on to the fund at a premium to net asset value that would otherwise make me a seller. Even when the fund is far from a buy (or DRIP) it can be considered a hold.

At a 30% premium, with a distribution ratio of 15% (roughly the current level) the fund generates a return on net assets of around ~3% just through issuing shares. That’s provided all shareholders would drip (which they don’t as not all brokers offer it).

The DRIP appears to take value from all shareholders and benefit the DRIP shareholders. However, to get this “benefit” of overpaying less, the DRIP shareholder is consistently buying assets at a massive premium to NAV.

Ultimately, the distribution seems unsustainable to me. It forces the fund to distribute more and more cash or shares that are unlikely to be generated through returns on the underlying.

If we don’t consider the tailwinds/headwinds of the drip program for a second.

The fund is trading at 130% of net asset value with a ~15% distribution yield. The management team has to generate a 19.5% return on assets under management.

That seems incredibly challenging with a (mostly) fixed income portfolio with a yield-to-maturity of 7.83% and a leverage ratio of 15.63%.

At a higher premium, the question is less and less about the attractiveness of the underlying and the outlook for fixed income, or whether the management team can deliver enough alpha to warrant the premium (highly unlikely) and it becomes increasingly important how much the DRIP program benefits you. Of the latest distribution of ~$0.18 per share, 68% was classified as a return of capital.

GOF has never traded above 38% of its net asset value. It has traded at a significant discount to NAV at times. The average premium is around 9%. A very high and impressive premium given most funds tend to trade at a discount and the average “premium” is negative.

Personally, I’d at least turn off the drip at these valuations. The risk of turning the drip program off is that NAV increases further or remains steady while other shareholders dilute you by enjoying the discount. Here are three examples of future scenarios:

Future Premium/Discount

Likely better total return

Reverts to average premium of 9%

Cash dividend

Premium stays 30%+

DRIP

Distribution Cut Premium Crash

Cash dividend

I acknowledge that there is a path where having the DRIP turned on is quite beneficial. However, it seems like a narrow path. Keep in mind that once the premium starts converging towards NAV the calculations change. Once we’re near ~106% of NAV I’ll be much less opposed to having the DRIP turned on.

Meanwhile, the average closed-end fund trades at a 4% discount. The average fixed-income fund trades at a 2.66% discount. This fund is clearly much better than average. There is a strong team behind it. It has a wide-ranging mandate and it uses variable amounts of leverage (indicating there is consideration to it). Its historical performance has been excellent. I think this is either a hold or a sell. In the end, I'll put a hold rating on it. The Fed is signalling interest rate cuts. The premium to NAV is still going higher. I don't see why the premium would collapse in the near term. Keep in mind that it could happen. The large premium gives a lot of runway to a potential drawdown.

data.title: CPZ: Discount Narrowing Drives Strong Results, But Now Takes Away Its Appeal
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Calamos L/S Equity & Dynamic Income Trust (CPZ) provides investors with exposure to a portfolio split between equity and fixed-income investments. In the fixed-income sleeve, it is primarily allocated to high-yield corporate debt, but can also include investment-grade corporate debt, syndicated loans, securitized debt, and preferred shares.

Setting this fund apart from other hybrid funds is that they also utilize a short-selling strategy within the fund. That's actually similar to the Clough Global Opportunities Fund (GLO), which we covered very recently.

Since our last update, the fund has performed incredibly well—which is great to see, even if it is a fund that I don't find particularly attractive. This is especially true as the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially in the last year. I maintain a "hold" rating, taking a more neutral stance on this fund.

CPZ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.97
  • Discount/Premium: -4.71%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.01%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.92%
  • Leverage: 25.62%
  • Managed Assets: $468.3 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date, November 25, 2031)

CPZ's investment objective is to "seek to provide current income and risk-managed capital appreciation." They also add that the fund "seeks to provide hedged market exposure built around Calamos' time-tested global long/short equity strategy." The portfolio fit they mention is, "the fund may be appropriate for risk-conscious investors who want to put capital to work in pursuit of income and capital appreciation."

The fund is leveraged, and that can increase overall risk due to amplified downside moves, though it does also allow for outsized upside moves when things are going well, too. When including the borrowing costs, the fund's total expense ratio comes in at 3.23%. The fund's expense ratio is on the higher side, even when excluding the leverage costs. This is because when they are selling short, there is an added expense of dividend and interest expense on those short sales.

Performance: Discount Narrowing

The fund has seen strong performance since our last update, beating out the S&P 500 Index—which is not a direct benchmark, but can provide overall context. With CPZ outperforming, that can be quite a feat during this time, considering it also includes short positions and fixed-income holdings.

With that said, while that is quite impressive, a lot of the results here were from the discount narrowing quite materially during this period. We can see exactly how much that has helped to boost the fund's total share price returns during this time when we split out the total NAV returns and compare that to its total share price return and SPY during the same time.

Of course, this is one of the benefits that comes with closed-end funds: the opportunity to potentially realize these sorts of large discounts. Ideally, we primarily look at relative historical discounts rather than the absolute discount of a fund alone. At some point, such a deep discount can make it an interesting choice if there is potential for activist pressure to push the fund to take actions to realize that discount.

Currently, CPZ is owned by well-known activist group Saba Capital Management and Sit Investments Associates, both owning about 5% currently. Saba has held a position for years now, and the latest move was trimming some of their holdings in late 2024. With the discount narrowing now to mid-single digits, it becomes less of a tempting opportunity on that front. If the fund dropped to a double-digit level again, though, it would certainly make it more interesting.

Distribution: Steady Monthly Payout

Since the fund's inception in late 2019, they have bumped up their payout a few times initially. The final time was in mid-2021, when the regular monthly distribution has been since. More recently, they paid out a rather large year-end special last year. The payout currently works out to a distribution rate of 10.01%, with a NAV distribution rate of 9.53%.

Since the fund's inception, they've paid a total cumulative $9.368.

To cover the distribution, CPZ will rely primarily on being able to realize gains from its underlying portfolio. That isn't all that uncommon, particularly for a fund that is tilted toward a heavy equity sleeve combined with a short sale strategy.

Net investment income coverage in the latest reported semi-annual posting came in at 16.5%. That was down from the last fiscal year, which was closer to 28%.

For tax purposes, the fund classified its 2024 distributions as a combination of qualified dividends, ordinary income, and short-term capital gains. The largest of these was short-term capital gains, which can make it less tax-friendly and more appropriate for a tax-sheltered account.

A small portion this year has also been estimated to be return of capital, which is generally tax-friendly as a way to defer tax obligations. Still, the largest characterization is expected to be STCG.

CPZ's Portfolio

More broadly, CPZ's exposure is primarily tilted toward its long/short component, at 70.7% of the portfolio. The preferred component stands at 13.7%, and the fixed-income sleeve is 15.6%. Within the long/short, the fund is tilted toward more long positions.

The largest positions on the long side include several of the Magnificent 7 names, with Amazon (AMZN) as the largest, NVIDIA (NVDA) coming in as the second largest, and then Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) also making an appearance.

Interestingly, when looking at the short side of the portfolio, they are primarily shorting the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

This is part of my main hesitation with finding CPZ appealing. The largest holding in SPY is NVDA these days; AMZN and GOOGL are also included, and those three positions account for 14.1% of SPY currently. Meaning that while they are long these positions, they are then shorting them indirectly as well. They are also showing a short Apple (AAPL) position, which is another 6.33% of SPY, meaning they are essentially short to a larger degree than the 1.1% listed above.

With that said, when the market pulls back or corrects, as we saw in April of this year, this fund acts as a hedge to the downside. We saw that play out in the above total NAV return chart as well. SPY declined meaningfully, and CPZ outperformed during that time. On the flip side, the total NAV returns gave up a lot of the recovery—as we would expect to see.

Conclusion

CPZ provides a hedge against the overall market given its short exposure. Shorting takes a bit of timing to do successfully, but CPZ is consistently short regularly. Meaning they aren't necessarily looking to manage their short position actively, but provide regular short exposure on a regular basis, primarily via SPY. At least that has been the case from what we've seen now covering this fund over the years. I believe that's what makes it a relatively less appealing fund to consider unless it trades at an outsized discount, which is not currently the case.

data.title: Sell Super Micro Computer Stock At Your Own Risk (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI), aka Supermicro stock, is flying in pre-market trading on Friday, up around 6%.

Supermicro has begun large-scale shipments of Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell Ultra systems and rack-level plug-and-play data center solutions, announcing on September 11, 2025, that it is now delivering Nvidia HGX B300 systems and GB300 NVL72 at volume to customers worldwide. By offering fully pre-validated solutions across systems, racks, and complete data center environments through its Data Center Building Block Solutions, the company enables day-one deployment with fewer integration delays. The company rolled out new AI systems built on Nvidia’s Blackwell back in June to speed up AI factory setups in Europe, with over 10 models now available.

With a portfolio of more than 10 SKUs, featuring both Nvidia Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra platforms, Supermicro provides customers with the flexibility to build diverse AI factory environments at virtually any scale, positioning itself as a top supplier in the market. The only question that remains is, can Supermicro sustain revenue growth?

According to the company's president and CEO, Charles Liang:

Supermicro has the best track record of fast and successful deployments of new Nvidia technologies…Through Supermicro Data Center Building Block Solutions with our expertise in on-site deployment, we enable turn-key delivery of the highest-performance AI platform — critical for customers seeking to invest in cutting-edge technology. Data center customers face many AI infrastructure challenges: complex network topology and cabling, power delivery, and thermal management. Supermicro delivers pre-validated, plug-and-play solutions at system, rack, and data center scale, enabling AI factories to deploy rapidly and helping our customers lead in AI.

The old and the new:

I last covered Supermicro following its fiscal Q4 earnings in early August, where weaker EPS and guidance sparked a 16% pullback despite continued strength in revenue growth and FY26 targets. Since then, shares have slipped another 6%. While margin pressures and high expectations remain challenges, I am upgrading the stock to a Strong Buy and continue to view the recent weakness as a buying opportunity given sustained AI-driven demand and attractive valuation multiples. Backed by its global manufacturing scale and strong hyperscaler relationships, the company is still positioned for more than 50% revenue growth in FY26 as AI data center demand accelerates.

While the stock is up around 44% year to date, against the S&P 500 (SP500), up 12.2%, I am continuing to recommend it at current levels.

The news further proves Supermicro’s ties with Nvidia, whose GPUs are the backbone of AI computing, despite intensified competition. I liked Supermicro at $35, and I liked it at $46, and I continue to like it at $43.

Risks

But I was wary of the stock back in mid-May.

I downgraded Super Micro to a Sell in mid-May after a 40% run, noting that the Saudi DataVolt deal had already been priced into the stock. Shares have traded roughly flat since. My concern was around unsold H100 GPU inventory and ongoing margin pressures, which made near-term financial improvement difficult. Furthermore, at the time, the company’s FY26 revenue target of $40 billion appeared overly ambitious and at risk of downward revision, with management still showing limited confidence and visibility at the time. Most of these stand, but my view has shifted as the risk/reward profile has improved.

With the stock consolidating after months of sideways action, I now see an attractive entry point. In my view, Supermicro is positioned to surprise to the upside as execution and visibility into FY26 improve, making the case for my renewed bullish stance. I think the pre-market action shows that investors are beginning to recognize the strength of Supermicro’s positioning as Nvidia’s closest AI hardware partner. The company’s ability to rapidly ship next-generation Blackwell Ultra systems at scale is a clear competitive advantage, and as AI infrastructure spending continues to accelerate, I expect Supermicro to capture a disproportionate share of that growth, as I discussed in detail in my last coverage.

The risk is there, but the stock’s muted performance since May has reset expectations, creating a favorable setup for a re-rating higher, in my opinion. To keep a long story short, sell Super Micro at your own risk.

Valuation:

Supermicro’s valuation remains cheap at current levels. If Supermicro sustains revenue growth ahead of the broader sector, which I believe it will, and simultaneously expands into higher-margin offerings like DCBBS amid the ongoing AI boom, the stock looks significantly undervalued. According to the company’s Senior Vice President of Corporate Development, Michael Staiger, at Citi's 2025 Global Technology, Media, and Telecommunications Conference last week:

With respect to sovereigns, there are licensing elements. It's a factor, like, have they had the licenses, can they ship? Have they spec-ed out the data center, right? So that kind of goes back to what we are seeing, what we're doing with DCBBS, where we can take — we can retrofit a data center for them, bring the whole experience together. We have the leading edge from a liquid-cooled element.

Looking at only the forward valuation metrics, Supermicro is trading at a forward P/E of 16.84, about 31% below the sector median, and a forward GAAP P/E of 19.70, nearly 38% lower. Its PEG Non-GAAP forward ratio sits at 0.65, a steep discount to the sector’s 1.82. This screams undervalued to me.

Price movement:

The stock is currently consolidating under the EMA200 at $44.6, and if it can reclaim and hold above $45 with volume, I think we could see a path towards +$50. Support is currently around $39-$40, and a breakdown below that risks revisiting the $34 level. RSI is recovering from oversold territory and is turning bullish, currently at 48. Recent volume spikes align with selloffs, and the bounce has come on lighter volume so far. Ideally, I want to see stronger volume to confirm a reversal. This is a make-or-break spot, and I’m betting on the former.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Its Energy Empire Is Better Than The Rock Of The Midstream
data.description:
Introduction

I have been following the Midstream industry since 2021, when most valuations in the sphere became depressed due to the ongoing COVID-19 crisis. I believe that the industry is one of the most fascinating and interesting for income-focused investors. The stability of the businesses means that they’re perfect for distribution and they are less reliant on the underlying commodity prices compared to upstream or downstream companies.

I’ve covered many companies in the field, and I now want to do a heads-up comparison with what I believe could be the best two options in the midstream segment: Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD). The two offer different value propositions, and I believe that they will be great for different sets of investors.

A quick reminder

I will review two MLPs in this analysis, which are fundamentally different from regular C-corporations like Apple Inc. (AAPL) or Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM). They have different tax treatments, as you’re not technically a shareholder when investing in an MLP but a limited partner that holds units in the structure. These partnerships don’t technically pay corporate tax, but instead, the profits and losses are passed through to the unitholders.

Unitholders have to file a K-1 form that is more complex than a simple 1099-DIV and breaks down your share of the partnership, credits and deductions. Though the distributions are considered Return of Capital, which is not directly taxed as income. It lowers your cost basis and allows you to defer taxes until you sell your units. There are, therefore, some considerations to invest in the MLP space as it adds tax complexity to a unitholders portfolio.

Energy Transfer: The Energy Empire

I’ve covered Energy Transfer a couple of times and it has been one of the longest holdings of mine and has rewarded me greatly over the years with both unit price appreciation as well as great distribution. It is one of the largest energy companies in the country and has exposure to absolutely all types of fossil fuel, from refined products to LNG to crude. It also includes strategic investments in Sunoco and USA Compression Partners, diversifying its presence in the energy value chain.

More recently, ET has increased its CapEx targets as it intends to invest over $5B in organic growth projects in 2025, which is significantly up from the past couple of years, as the company expects increased US electricity demand, mainly in Texas, where ET has focused on connecting its assets to various power plants. ET is also facing strong demand from Data centers that want to bypass utilities and directly contract energy supply.

Energy Transfer has a vast set of assets with an extensive pipeline network that reaches around 140,000 miles connecting all major US production basins to key demand centers as well as export hubs on the Gulf Coast.

Regarding natural gas, the company has an intricate system of both interstate and intrastate pipelines that includes the Transwestern, which is currently undergoing an increase in capacity from the Permian. The Rover Pipeline is also critical to transporting gas from the Marcellus and Utica shale deposits. The Intrastate network is also a key component of its portfolio, and I believe that the company’s interstate segment offers a great upside to Energy Transfer, as the segment offers huge barriers to entry and massive scale advantages.

For crude oil, some key assets include the well-known Dakota Access Pipeline as well as ETCOP, which connects the Bakken Shale to various storage hubs and refineries. The Permian Express has also become key to ET’s operation, tapping into the prolific Permian Basin and shipping crude to the Gulf Coast markets. NGLs and refined products include the Mariner East Pipeline system as well as the Lone Star NGL pipeline, which connect the Marcellus/Utica shales and the Permian Basin to Mont Belvieu, respectively.

ET also has a massive, strictly midstream operation that includes the Nederland Terminal, which is a world-class facility on the Gulf Coast and handles both the import and export of ET’s petroleum products. Other assets in the category include Mont Belvieu and various crude storage facilities across the country.

If we dig into what makes Energy Transfer really special, the energy empire has an unmatched scale that is now highly diversified, which can provide a certain hedge to a singular commodity weakness. Regarding risk, ET has been strengthening its balance sheet in order to achieve an investment-grade credit rating. And this combines with a more disciplined capital allocation that has been the previous problem for ET, as it used to do a lot of deals to expand its operation, which has been a tad aggressive in the past. With the focus on organic growth, I believe that ET could be even more rewarding towards unitholders. Furthermore, I believe that ET’s management is world-class, as it has been able to build an unparalleled infrastructure footprint.

Enterprise Products Partners: The Rock of the Midstream

EPD is also a titan in the midstream energy industry as it is a leading provider of natural gas, NGLs, crude, and other petroleum-derived products. It is well-renowned for its financial discipline and conservative management, which has helped the company navigate even the most tumultuous times of the industry. And compared to ET, EPD is more focused on being stable and generating constant DCF instead of focusing on controlling the whole energy midstream segment. The fee-based model of the company allows it to protect its earnings from direct commodity price volatility.

EPD’s management recently discussed its capital priorities, and with its mix of contracted revenue, we should see even more return to unitholders as EPD should limit its growth capital in the near future. Figures are suggesting a potential 71% of DCF being redistributed to unitholders. Though the company is focusing mostly on its NGL segment, with Permian gas and various NGL processing facilities. The firm is also expanding its export infrastructure in the Gulf as US NGL exports have been running at full speed over the past couple of years. This will also be coupled with rising NGL production as wells tend to produce more gas and NGLs as they get older.

The NGL P/S operation is certainly EPD’s biggest value proposition, as it captures most of the supply chain, offering great advantages for customers. Furthermore, NGLs tend to generate more fees than regular crude gathering because they are under more advantageous terms. I believe that this segment has an enormous moat, as EPD has direct access to international markets with its marine terminals. This allows Enterprise to either sell in the US or on international markets, depending on prices, allowing the company to directly pick the best potential margins.

The company’s crude segment is also highly robust, possessing some of the most crucial pipelines in the country, tapping into both the Permian and Cushing. Some key assets include the Midland-to-ECHO pipeline system as well as the Seaway Pipeline system, which are seeing high utilization rates due to their crucial positioning. EPD also has a natural gas segment that consists of intrastate networks in Louisiana and Texas. The contracts are mostly fixed-fee with no take-or-pay provisions that could reduce overall profitability for the segment.

Now I believe that the company will continue to maintain its financial fortress thanks to its peer-leading credit rating while expanding capacity on current routes, with measures such as the temporary conversion of the Midland-to-ECHO 2 while the company builds the Bahia pipeline. It is important to note that generally EPD focuses on funding growth via its own retained DCF instead of the excess use of debt, which would leave it more vulnerable in a down cycle.

I also believe that EPD has an unmatched NGL dominance with its massive integrated network that has a “wellhead-to-water” control over the entire value chain, creating great economies of scale and a highly competitive moat hard to replicate by other competitors. The company's balance sheet is set as a fortress, capable of weathering any financial storm, akin to the approach of JPMorgan in the banking industry, in my opinion. I think that’s why so many income-focused investors like the company, especially with its long history of consecutive annual distribution increases.

Valuation

Both ET and EPD have relatively in line valuation metrics compared to the broad industry, with an EV/EBITDA of 9.15 and 10.7, respectively, while ET has the advantage in terms of FCF yield at 9.4%, while EPD has a current 6.1% yield. EPD tends to generally command higher multiples as the business is deemed safer than Energy Transfer’s, as investors tend to put a premium on predictability. ET is broadly more complex and tends to have a higher leverage. Though I believe that ET has drastically changed its way of doing business and shunned aggressive acquisitions, paying down debt.

Regarding growth, the two companies differ with ET seeking transformative projects such as the recently approved Lake Charles LNG export facility, which could become another highly valuable income stream for the company and become a multi-billion dollar project that could reshape ET. On the other hand, EPD has the stable approach of incremental, low-risk investments, such as bolt-ons, like the acquisitions of certain assets from Western Midstream back in late 2024. EPD is also banking on its Petrochemicals segment, with expansion towards its downstream activity with projects such as the PDH plants that will add higher-value products such as Propylene. ET has been able to grow its EBITDA at a higher rate than EPD while its revenue has posted negative CAGR growth over the past 3 years due to ET’s revenue mix that has a larger component of its operation related to commodity-based contracts in which the company purchases and resells underlying energy commodities. Thus, the swing in prices has had a greater effect on ET compared to EPD. Regarding EBITDA, ET’s acquisitions, such as Crestwood and Enable, were directly accretive to the company’s profitability.

Both ET and EPD have similar margins despite having different approaches, as it resembles more of a Quality vs. Quantity dynamic. EPD is really a value chain integrator, as highlighted by its deep integration of its NGL segment and has been able to move higher-margin products. Regarding ET, it could be compared to the highway of fossil fuels with segments in each possible midstream operation. Though despite higher volumes, the transportation of natural and crude oil is essentially a “toll road” business that is generally lower-margin. Regarding return metrics, ET has slightly lower ones as it pursues more deals than EPD, thus depreciating metrics such as ROIC.

For distribution, ET currently has a higher yield at 7.5% compared to EPD’s 6.8% though EPD has a stronger track record with an unbroken 25+ year streak of consecutive increases. EPD is highly predictable in terms of the reliability of distribution that investors expect throughout any economic cycles. While ET famously cut its distribution to unitholders back in 2020 to redirect cash flow towards strengthening the balance sheet during the tumultuous times of the COVID period, which certainly offered a great opportunity to buy units. ET has since drastically increased its distribution to a higher level than pre-2020, as it achieved its leverage targets.

EPD has a better balance sheet and a better grasp on debt levels as it posts better overall metrics. As mentioned earlier, EPD has a great credit rating at A- from S&P, which allows Enterprise to have lower interest payments, and it targets a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.5x. ET has been improving its credit rating over the past 4 years to now reach BBB, and the midstream company also has a leverage target range of 4x-4.5x debt-to-EBITDA, which the company has been successful in reaching.

Conclusion

With this full analysis behind us, I’ll start by saying that both of these companies are great; in fact, I rated them both as Strong Buys in previous analyses, and I still rate them as such. But the hard part comes now: which one of Energy Transfer or Enterprise Products Partners is the best company to invest in? I believe that EPD is a better value for retirees who rely on conservative and safe income, while ET offers greater total return potential and goes more towards value investor profiles. In general, I give the advantage to Energy Transfer, as I believe that it could offer the best growth prospects heading forward.

Risks

The two of them are large infrastructure-based companies that are operating in an industry that is currently being challenged by renewables. If the transition were to accelerate, this would reduce throughput volumes and hurt overall revenue. Furthermore, the two midstream companies would incur significant write-offs of their large asset base as well.

The risk of commodity price swings is limited, as midstream companies are dependent on volumes and not necessarily prices, though if their customers were to go bankrupt, this would significantly hurt them. ET is slightly more exposed than EPD to this kind of risk.
